era_context: The Roman Empire under Severus Alexander (222–235 CE) faces mounting pressure on multiple frontiers. The Sassanid Persian Empire under Ardashir I (r. 224–241 CE) is consolidating power after overthrowing the Parthian Arsacid dynasty. In China, the Three Kingdoms period (220–280 CE) is fragmenting the Han legacy; the state of Wei dominates the north under the Cao family. Germanic tribes press the Rhine and Danube frontiers. This is an era of imperial strain across Eurasia.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Mesoamerica (no written chronology for this period)

events.title: Sassanid invasion of Mesopotamia and Roman counteroffensive
events.description: Ardashir I of the Sassanid Empire launches a major invasion of Roman Mesopotamia, capturing several cities including Carrhae and Nisibis. This represents the first major test of Sassanid military power under the new dynasty. Roman Emperor Severus Alexander responds with a counteroffensive, though sources differ on the outcome. The campaign establishes the Sassanid-Roman frontier as a zone of chronic conflict that will persist for four centuries. Ancient sources emphasize the threat posed by Sassanid expansion to Roman eastern hegemony.
key_figures: Praetorian Prefect Ulpian (advisor, though he died c. 228)

sources.name: Herodian, History of the Roman Empire
Dio Cassius, Roman History (via Xiphilinus epitome)
Sassanid inscriptions (Res Gestae Divi Saporis, later compilation but referencing Ardashir's campaigns)

events.certainty_note: The invasion is attested in multiple sources, but precise dating to 230 vs. 231 is debated. Herodian places it during Severus Alexander's reign; exact year varies by source.

events.title: Wei Dynasty consolidation under Cao Pi's successors
events.description: The state of Wei, founded by Cao Cao and formally established as a dynasty by his son Cao Pi (r. 220–226), continues under Cao Rui (r. 226–239). Cao Rui strengthens administrative structures and military organization, consolidating Wei's dominance over northern China. This period sees the development of the 'Nine Rank System' (Jiupin Zhongzheng) for bureaucratic recruitment, which will influence Chinese governance for centuries. Wei's control of the Yellow River valley and northern plains gives it economic and military advantage over rival states Wu and Shu.

sources.name: Sanguozhi (Records of the Three Kingdoms) by Chen Shou, compiled c. 280 CE
Zizhi Tongjian (Comprehensive Mirror for Aid in Government) by Sima Guang, 11th century compilation

events.certainty_note: Wei's political consolidation in this period is well-attested in Chinese historical records, though specific events within 230 are sometimes conflated with adjacent years.
cross_references: Leads to 239-001 (Cao Rui's death and succession crisis)

events.title: Germanic raids on Roman Rhine frontier
events.region: Rhine frontier, Germania, Roman provinces of Germania Superior and Inferior
events.description: Germanic tribes, including Alamanni and Franks, intensify raids across the Rhine into Roman territory. These incursions are part of broader Germanic migration and pressure on Roman frontiers during the 3rd century crisis. Severus Alexander's military resources are stretched between the Sassanid threat in the east and Germanic pressure in the north. Roman response is documented but often reactive rather than strategic. These raids foreshadow the larger Germanic movements of the later 3rd century.
key_figures: Germanic tribal leaders (names largely unrecorded in Roman sources)

sources.name: Herodian, History of the Roman Empire

events.certainty_note: Germanic raids on the Rhine are attested for this period, but specific dating to 230 is approximate. Herodian and later sources group such raids without precise chronology.
cross_references: Part of broader 3rd century Germanic migration pressure
Leads to 235-001 (Severus Alexander's assassination and military crisis)

disconfirming_evidence: The extent of Sassanid military success in 230 is debated. Some modern scholars argue that Herodian and later Roman sources exaggerate Sassanid victories to justify Roman military expenditure. The actual territorial gains by Ardashir may have been more limited than traditional accounts suggest. Additionally, the dating of the Sassanid invasion to 230 vs. 231–232 remains uncertain; some sources place it later in Severus Alexander's reign.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 230 CE is moderate but heavily weighted toward Roman and Chinese sources. Roman accounts come primarily from Herodian (writing c. 240 CE, not contemporary) and later compilations like the Historia Augusta (4th century, unreliable). Chinese records are more contemporary but focus on internal Wei politics. Sassanid sources are sparse and often later inscriptions. Sub-Saharan Africa, India, and Southeast Asia are largely absent from surviving documentation. The bias toward military and political events over economic or social history is pronounced.

graph_edges.note: Sassanid invasion follows from Ardashir I's consolidation of power after overthrowing Parthia
Sassanid-Roman conflict continues and escalates in subsequent years
Wei consolidation is part of the broader Three Kingdoms fragmentation
Germanic pressure contributes to military crisis leading to Severus Alexander's assassination
